title: practical english usage - Part 26
content:
6 passive      structures For details of this structure, ►    269.

She made it clear that she disagreed. (     She made that she disagreed clear.) She made her views clear.

there is an object complement, it is common to use it as a preparatory object.

When  the object of a verb is a clause, infinitive structure or -ing structure, and 5  introductory         it:  She    made       it  clear      that    ...

The testers found this bicycle to be the best value for money.

- I found the bicycle very comfortable to ride.

grammar *12  two-part verbs: phrasal verbs We put off the meeting till Tuesday. (Put off is not the same as put + off.) I looked the word up in the dictionary. (Look up is not the same as look + up.) Joe turned up last night. (= 'appeared’  - not the same as turned + up.) War broke out in 1939. (Broke out is not the same as broke + out.) two parts taken separately.

The  meaning of a phrasal verb is often very different from the meanings of the 2 idiomatic       meanings:      break       out;   turn     up For a detailed comparison, ►     19 5.

I jumped off the wall. (preposition) I switched the light off. (adverb particle) Some of these words can also be used as prepositions. Compare: back, by, down, forward, in, home, off, on, out, over, past, through, up.

Common adverb particles: about, across, ahead, along, (a)round, aside, away, Get back! She walked out. I switched the light off.

two-part verbs are often called 'phrasal verbs’ .

Many English verbs can be followed by small adverbs ('adverb particles’ ). These 1  verb    + adverb      particle:     get    back,     walk      out 12 two-part      verbs:   phrasal      verbs   ___________ See also the entries for particular linking verbs.

For cases like drive slow, think positive, ►     194.2, 194.4.       For more about structures after verbs, ►     16.

For the difference between adjectives and adverbs, ►     193.

He pulled his belt tight and started off. (     ... tightly...) not New SUPER GUB washes clothes SUPER WHITE. (     .. .WHITELY...) describe the object of the verb.

Adjectives can also be used in the structure verb + object + adjective, to not He fell unconscious on the floor. (     .. .unconsciously-...) She sat motionless, waiting for their decision.

The valley lay quiet and peaceful in the sun.

verb. It is common in descriptions with sit, stand, lie, fall.

we  are really describing the subject of the sentence, and not the action of the Sometimes other verbs, too, can be followed by adjectives. This happens when 5  other     verbs     followed       by   adjectives For the differences between these verbs, ►    394.

Keep calm.           His hair is turning grey.

How does she stay so young? I hope you will always remain so happy.

It's growing late.        The leaves are going brown.

It's becoming colder.      I'm getting sleepy.

The  most common are: become, get, grow, go, turn, stay, remain, keep.

Some linking verbs are used to talk about change, or the absence of change.

4 change:     become,        get,    grow,      etc grammar *13   two-part verbs: prepositional verbs I get along with her quite well.  Stop talking and get on with your work. -» (making them three-part verbs).

A  few verbs can be used with both an adverb particle (►   12) and a preposition 4 verbs     with    prepositions         and   particles       together style than ... the book for which I was looking.) I've found the book which I was looking for. (More natural in an informal not What are you thinking about? (     About what are you thinking?) other preposition-final structures, ►    209.

separated from its object and go at the end of the clause. For details of this and clause), a prepositional verb usually stays together, so that a preposition can be When  an object comes at the beginning of a clause (e.g. in a question or relative 3 word    order:    What      are    you     thinking         about?

He took ages to get over his illness. (Get over is not the same as get + over.) look + after.) Could you look after the kids while I'm out? (Look after is not the same as the two parts taken separately.
